title: C-Bet on a Dry Ace-High Board
actionHistory: You raised to 25 from CO, BB called. Flop Ac-7h-2s.

options.optionExplanation: Small c-bet on a dry board where you have range advantage and top pair top kicker. Ideal sizing.
Checking with top pair on a board that heavily favors your range gives up too much value.
Betting big on a dry board is overkill. You want to bet small at high frequency here.

explanation: This is a textbook c-bet spot. You're the preflop raiser on an A-7-2 rainbow board — one of the driest, most range-advantageous boards possible for the raiser. Your range contains way more aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT) than the BB's calling range. Because you have such a strong range advantage, you should c-bet at a very high frequency with a small sizing (about 1/3 pot). The small bet is all you need — it puts pressure on the BB's weak hands while risking little. Villain has to fold hands like KQ, KJ, QJ, small pairs that missed. Save your big bets for boards where you need to charge draws.
keyConceptSummary: On dry ace-high boards, the preflop raiser has huge range advantage. C-bet small (1/3 pot) at high frequency.
villainRange: BB defend: A8-A2, 77, 22, 87s, suited connectors that missed

title: Checking Back a Wet Coordinated Board
actionHistory: You raised to 25 from BTN, BB called. Flop 9h-8h-7c.

options.optionExplanation: Your AK missed completely on a board that heavily favors the caller's range. Check back and reevaluate.
C-betting into a 9-8-7 board with AK high is lighting money on fire. BB has tons of pairs, straights, and draws.
A larger c-bet here is even worse. The BB's range crushes this board.

explanation: This is where many beginners go wrong — they c-bet every flop. On 9h-8h-7c, the BB's calling range absolutely dominates this board. The BB has hands like T9, 98, 87, 76, T8, JT, 65, suited hearts, pocket pairs 66-99 — all of which either hit the board or have huge draws. Your AK has nothing but two overcards (6 outs, ~24% equity). Meanwhile, your c-bet will rarely fold out BB's pairs and draws. Checking back preserves your equity, lets you see a free turn card, and avoids building a pot you'll likely lose. Not every flop deserves a c-bet. The question is always: does my range advantage justify betting?
keyConceptSummary: Don't auto-c-bet every flop. On wet, connected boards that favor the caller's range, checking back with weak hands is correct.
villainRange: BB defend: 98, 87, T9, 76, JT, 65, 99-66, suited hearts, overcards

title: C-Bet Bluff on King-High Dry Board
actionHistory: You raised to 25 from CO, BB called. Flop Kc-5h-2d.

options.optionExplanation: Small c-bet bluff on a dry king-high board. Your range has many kings and this bet folds out BB's missed hands.
You have two overcards and backdoor straight draws. Checking isn't terrible but misses a profitable bluffing opportunity.
Right idea to bet, but the sizing is too large for a dry board bluff.

explanation: Even though you missed the flop, this is a strong c-bet opportunity. K-5-2 rainbow is another dry, raiser-favoring board. Your raising range from CO has lots of kings (AK, KQ, KJ, KT) while the BB's range mostly missed. By betting small (1/3 pot), you only need this bluff to work about 25% of the time to break even. BB has to fold all their unpaired hands — QJ, QT, JT, suited connectors, small pocket pairs below 5 — which is a huge portion of their range. Notice you also have QJ with two overcards and a gutshot to a straight (any T), so even if called, you have about 20% equity. This is what a good c-bet bluff looks like: high fold equity, low risk, some backup equity.
keyConceptSummary: C-bet bluffs work best on dry boards that favor the raiser's range. Small sizings need to work less often to be profitable.
villainRange: BB defend: Kx, 55, 22, suited connectors, small pairs

title: Double Barrel — Turn Bet After Flop C-Bet
actionHistory: You raised CO, BB called. Flop Kd-8c-3s, you bet 20, BB called. Turn Ac.

options.optionExplanation: You improved to top pair on a scare card. Double barrel for value — BB's flop calling range is now in trouble.
Checking top pair on a scare card turn misses significant value from worse hands.
Betting pot is too large. A 2/3 pot bet extracts value while keeping worse hands in.

explanation: This is a great double barrel spot for two reasons. First, you improved — the Ac gave you top pair with a great kicker. Second, the ace is a scare card for the BB. On the flop, BB called with a range that includes kings, eights, draws, and speculative hands. The ace on the turn is terrible for most of that range — anyone with K8, K5, K4, pocket pairs below aces is now worried they're behind. Your 2/3 pot bet puts maximum pressure on these hands while still getting value from AK, A8, and stubborn Kx hands. This illustrates the concept of 'barreling scare cards' — when a turn card is better for your perceived range than your opponent's, it's a great spot to fire again.
keyConceptSummary: Double barrel when the turn card improves your hand or is a scare card that favors your range over your opponent's calling range.
villainRange: BB flop call: Kx, 88, 33, A8, gutshots — now many are second-best

title: Multiway Pot — Tighten Up Your C-Bet Range
actionHistory: You raised to 25 from UTG, CO called, BB called. Flop Th-7d-4c.

options.optionExplanation: Top pair top kicker is strong enough to bet for value in a multiway pot, but use a moderate sizing.
Checking isn't terrible multiway, but AT on T-7-4 is strong enough to bet for value.
Full pot is too aggressive multiway. One of two opponents likely has something to continue with.

explanation: Multiway pots require a completely different c-betting approach than heads-up. With two opponents, you need a stronger hand to bet because someone is more likely to have connected with the board. However, AT on T-7-4 is strong enough — you have top pair top kicker, which beats most hands in both opponents' ranges. The key adjustment is your frequency: in a heads-up pot, you might c-bet 70% of the time on this board. Multiway, you should c-bet only your strong hands — top pair good kicker or better. Your bluff frequency drops dramatically because bluffs need to get through multiple players. AT here is a value bet, not a bluff, so fire away with a moderate sizing.
keyConceptSummary: In multiway pots, c-bet less often but with stronger hands. Bluffing into multiple opponents is less effective.
villainRange: CO + BB ranges: sets, two pair, Tx, 77, 44, draws, overcards

title: Monotone Flop — Checking Back Without a Flush Draw
actionHistory: You raised to 25 from BTN, BB called. Flop Jh-8h-4h.

options.optionExplanation: On a monotone board without a flush draw, checking back is correct. You have no heart and limited equity.
C-betting into a monotone board without a flush draw is reckless. BB has many flushes and flush draws.
An even larger bet on this board is throwing chips away. You're rarely getting folds from hands that beat you.

explanation: Monotone boards (all one suit) are dangerous for the raiser when you don't have the relevant suit. On Jh-8h-4h, the BB's calling range is full of hearts — any two hearts is a flush, and any single heart is a draw. Your AKd has zero hearts, meaning you have just two overcards with no backdoor flush draw. C-betting here accomplishes nothing positive: you won't fold out flush draws or made flushes, and you're building a pot where you're often drawing dead. Check back, hope to hit an A or K on the turn (6 outs, ~13%), and reassess. Board texture should drive your c-betting decisions as much as your actual hand.
keyConceptSummary: On monotone boards, avoid c-betting without the relevant suit. The caller's range is heavily weighted toward flushes and flush draws.
villainRange: BB on monotone: any flush, any heart draw, Jx, 88, 44

title: Paired Board — High Frequency Small C-Bet
actionHistory: You raised to 25 from CO, BB called. Flop 8c-8h-3d.

options.optionExplanation: Small c-bet on a paired board. Neither player likely has an 8, and your overcards have decent equity. Bet small at high frequency.
Not bad, but you miss a profitable opportunity. On paired boards, small c-bets have very high success rates.
Too large. On a paired board where neither player connects often, small bets work best.

explanation: Paired boards are unique for c-betting strategy. On 8-8-3, neither player is likely to have an 8 (it's a specific card), which means the board is essentially 'unconnected' for both players. The preflop raiser still has range advantage because they have more overpairs and big cards. A tiny c-bet (25-33% pot) puts your opponent in an awkward spot: they have to call with nothing hoping to improve, or fold and surrender the pot. Your KQ has two overcards, giving you good equity even if called. The principle: on dry paired boards, c-bet very small at very high frequency — it's one of the most profitable c-bet situations in poker.
keyConceptSummary: Paired boards favor the preflop raiser because neither player usually has trips. Bet very small at high frequency.
villainRange: BB defend: rarely 8x, pocket pairs, suited connectors, broadways

title: Out of Position — C-Betting as the 3-Bettor
actionHistory: BTN raised to 25, you 3-bet to 80 from BB, BTN called. Flop Jc-7d-4s.

options.optionExplanation: Small c-bet in a 3-bet pot with an overpair. You have range advantage as the 3-bettor on this board.
QQ is strong enough to bet for value. Checking gives BTN a free card and information.
Too large in a 3-bet pot where stacks are getting shorter. Small bets are more efficient.

explanation: In 3-bet pots, the 3-bettor has a significant range advantage on most boards because their range is stronger (QQ+ and AK type hands vs the caller's wider range). On J-7-4, your QQ is an overpair that beats most of villain's range. Even though you're out of position, you should c-bet because: (1) your hand is strong, (2) your range is strong on this board, and (3) the pot is already large so you want to start building toward stacks. The key adjustment in 3-bet pots is sizing — bet smaller (about 1/3 pot) because the pot is already inflated and stacks are shallower. A 40-chip bet into 120 accomplishes the same goal as a bigger bet on a smaller pot.
keyConceptSummary: In 3-bet pots, the 3-bettor has strong range advantage on most boards. Use smaller c-bet sizings because the pot is already large.
villainRange: BTN flat 3-bet: TT-66, AJs, ATs, KQs, JTs, T9s — mostly medium strength

title: Giving Up on the Turn — When to Stop Barreling
actionHistory: You raised CO, BB called. Flop Kd-9c-3s, you bet 20, BB called. Turn 6d.

options.optionExplanation: Your flop bluff didn't work. The turn doesn't improve you or scare your opponent. Time to give up.
Double barreling with ace-high on a blank turn against a calling range is spewy.
Firing big with nothing on a turn that doesn't change the story is burning chips.

explanation: You c-bet the K-9-3 flop as a bluff with A5 hearts and got called. BB's calling range is now weighted toward kings, nines, and draws. The turn is a 6d — a complete blank that doesn't help you, doesn't complete any draws, and doesn't scare your opponent. This is where discipline matters. Your flop bluff represented strength, BB called anyway, and nothing has changed. Continuing to bet (double barreling) with no equity improvement, no new scare card, and no plan is just burning money. Good players know when to give up on a bluff. Save your turn bets for when: (1) you improve, (2) a scare card falls, or (3) you have a strong draw as backup equity.
keyConceptSummary: Don't double barrel just because you bluffed the flop. Continue betting only when the turn card improves your hand or your story.
villainRange: BB flop call range: Kx, 99, 98s, draws, A9, stubborn pairs

title: Delayed C-Bet — Checking Flop, Betting Turn
actionHistory: You raised BTN, BB called. Flop 9c-7h-4d, you checked back. Turn As.

options.optionExplanation: You checked a bad flop for your range, then hit the perfect turn card. Now bet for value with top pair top kicker.
Checking twice with TPTK gives up too much value. The ace changed everything.
Right idea but slightly too large. A 2/3 pot bet is more balanced here.

explanation: This demonstrates the powerful 'delayed c-bet' strategy. On the flop, 9-7-4 was a terrible board for your AKs — it heavily favors the caller's range, so you correctly checked back. But the turn ace completely transforms the situation. Now you have top pair top kicker on a board where BB likely doesn't have an ace (they would've expected you to c-bet the flop, so they're less suspicious of your check-bet line). The delayed c-bet catches opponents off guard: they think your check on the flop showed weakness, so they're more likely to call your turn bet with worse hands. This is more deceptive than c-betting the flop and potentially getting raised off your unmade hand.
keyConceptSummary: Delayed c-bets (check flop, bet turn) work well when you check a bad flop then improve on the turn. It's deceptive and profitable.
villainRange: BB after flop check-through: wide range of pairs, draws, now many second-best to your aces
